compile.output: No compiler errors or warnings. Compiled with -O3 -march=native -std=c++17 -Wall -lpthread.

run.output: Query executed successfully. Wall-clock time: 449ms (average of 3 runs: 456ms, 447ms, 449ms). Consistent performance across runs.

validation.comparison_result: 10 rows returned, all match ground truth exactly. Columns: l_orderkey, revenue (sum of l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount)), o_orderdate, o_shippriority. Example top result: (4791171, 440715.2185, 1995-02-23, 0).

profiling.note: perf not used. Code analysis: 449ms indicates good parallelism (48 threads), but still significant room for optimization. Estimated breakdowns: ~250ms lineitem scan + filtering, ~100ms hash operations, ~50ms aggregation merge, ~50ms sorting/output.

Q3.notes: Correct result set. MAJOR IMPROVEMENT from iter_1 (1898ms → 449ms = 4.23x speedup). Key optimizations applied: (1) Replaced std::unordered_map with custom FastHashTable (open-addressing), (2) Increased thread count to 48, (3) Eliminated nested loop joins (was storing vector of OrderRecords per orderkey, now single OrderInfo), (4) Improved hash operations efficiency. Still 4.7x slower than DuckDB (91ms), likely due to: lack of zone map filtering, possible sub-optimal threading overhead, and opportunities for SIMD acceleration on filtering/arithmetic.

Q3.bottleneck: Multi-level analysis: (1) Lineitem scan (60M rows) with two predicates (l_shipdate > cutoff AND orderkey in filtered set) — no block-level filtering despite available zone maps on l_shipdate; full 60M rows processed despite ~33% selectivity. (2) Hash table operations dominate aggregation phase — FastHashTable is faster than std::unordered_map but still has O(1) probe chains on misses. (3) Thread pool at 48 threads vs 64 available cores leaves ~25% parallelism potential unused. (4) No SIMD vectorization for filters or arithmetic (l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount)). (5) Possible memory bandwidth saturation due to streaming full lineitem + orders columns without cache locality optimization.
Q3.root_cause: Iteration 1 was a semantic/algorithmic fix (removed nested loops in join processing). Iteration 2 focused on hash table performance and thread count. Root causes for remaining slowness: (A) Block-skipping not implemented — zone maps for l_shipdate could skip ~67% of 60M lineitem rows, but are loaded but unused. (B) Thread underutilization — 48 threads on 64-core system; with NUMA overhead, could still add 8-16 more threads. (C) Missing SIMD — filtering and revenue calculations are amenable to vectorization (AVX-512 available on hardware). (D) Lock-free merge not fully exploited — final aggregation merge could use atomic operations or thread-local-only design. (E) Hash function quality — current FNV-1a is reasonable but not optimized for tuple keys (composite keys may have poor distribution).

analysis.overall: Q3 iteration 2 is SIGNIFICANTLY improved from iteration 1 (4.23x speedup from 1898ms to 449ms) and CORRECT (10 rows match ground truth). The query is now dominated by: (1) Full-table lineitem scan with filtering (60M rows, ~200-250ms), (2) Hash join operations (orders_map lookups), (3) Aggregation and final merge. Compared to DuckDB (91ms avg), GenDB is 4.7x slower, indicating substantial remaining optimization potential. The implementation is well-structured with morsel-driven parallelism, custom open-addressing hash tables, and thread-local aggregation. Opportunities: (1) Zone map filtering to skip ~67% of lineitem blocks, (2) Thread count tuning to 56-60, (3) SIMD vectorization for filtering and arithmetic, (4) Possible improvements to hash function for composite tuple keys, (5) Micro-optimization of FastHashTable probe behavior. Conservative estimate: zone maps + SIMD + tuning could reduce to ~200-300ms (approaching DuckDB).

performance_optimizations.technique: Implement zone map filtering for l_shipdate column to skip blocks
performance_optimizations.description: Storage design provides sorted indexes and zone maps (storage_design.json lines 31-33). Lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate and has l_shipdate_sorted index. Current code does NOT use zone maps; it scans all 60M lineitem rows and filters each row. With zone maps: (1) Load zone map metadata from storage (min/max l_shipdate per 100K-row block). (2) Before processing each morsel in step 3 (process_lineitem_morsels), check if block's l_shipdate range overlaps (cutoff_shipdate, 1998-12-31]. (3) Skip blocks entirely if cutoff_shipdate >= block_max_l_shipdate. Implementation: Parse zone map metadata from lineitem directory (if available as binary file or JSON sidecar), build a block-skip list, modify the morsel-processing loop to skip marked morsels. Expected selectivity gain: ~67% of lineitem rows have l_shipdate ≤ cutoff_shipdate, so skip ~40M rows' I/O and processing.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~120-180ms reduction (25-40% of 449ms). Reasoning: (1) 60M lineitem rows → skip ~40M = process only 20M. (2) Each row involves mmap read + hash join lookup + arithmetic. Skipping 67% rows saves ~2/3 of that cost. (3) Zone maps also reduce L3 cache misses by avoiding unnecessary memory access. Conservative estimate: 25-40% overall speedup from zone maps alone.

performance_optimizations.technique: Add SIMD vectorization for filtering and revenue calculation
performance_optimizations.description: Hardware supports AVX-512 (lscpu output: avx512f, avx512dq, avx512cd, avx512bw, avx512vl). Current code processes rows scalar: (line 443) l_shipdate_data[i] > cutoff_shipdate, (line 444) l_extendedprice_data[i] * (1.0 - l_discount_data[i]). Vectorize: (1) Date comparisons: Load 8 int32_t l_shipdate values into __m512i, compare all 8 against cutoff in one instruction, extract mask. (2) Revenue calculation: Load 4 doubles into __m256d for extendedprice, 4 for discount, compute (price * (1 - discount)) for all 4 in parallel. (3) Combine predicates with bitwise AND/OR on masks. Replace lines 438-456 in process_lineitem_morsels with SIMD-vectorized loop. May need to use pragmas like `#pragma omp simd` or explicit `_mm512_cmpgt_epi32` calls depending on compiler version.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~40-70ms reduction (10-15% of 449ms). Reasoning: Filtering dominates scan cost (~250ms). SIMD processes 4-8 values per instruction cycle. Even with memory bandwidth limits, expect 4-6x speedup on filter operations. Filtering is ~55% of total time, so 5x speedup on filters = ~55% * 0.8 = 44% improvement = ~200ms reduction. Conservative (accounting for ILP/bandwidth limits): 10-15% = 45-67ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Increase thread count from 48 to 56-60 cores
performance_optimizations.description: Hardware has 64 cores (32 cores * 2 threads per core = 64 logical cores). Current code (line 292) caps thread pool at `std::min(48, hardware_concurrency)`. With 64 cores available, 48 threads leave 16 cores idle. Change to `std::min(60, std::thread::hardware_concurrency())` to better saturate hardware. This is particularly effective for the lineitem scan phase (which has high parallelism potential). Impact is smaller after zone map filtering is added (fewer rows to parallelize), so apply this optimization AFTER zone maps.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~15-30ms reduction (3-7% of 449ms). Reasoning: 48 threads on 64-core system = 75% core utilization. Increasing to 60 threads = 94% utilization. Lineitem scan phase (250ms, highly parallelizable) scales near-linearly. 25% thread increase (48 → 60) = 25% speedup on lineitem phase = 250 * 0.25 * 1/7 ≈ 9ms. With overhead reduction and merge improvements, estimate 15-30ms total savings.

performance_optimizations.technique: Optimize FastHashTable hash function for composite tuple keys
performance_optimizations.description: Current hash function for GroupKey (line 204-217) uses naive FNV-1a on tuple bytes: XOR each byte and multiply by FNV prime. For 3 int32_t values (12 bytes), this produces sequential operations without mixing. Better approach: (1) Use a high-quality hash function like XXH3 or MurmurHash for better distribution and less clustering. (2) Seed the hash with each tuple component separately to avoid sequential byte processing. (3) Consider using fastrange (https://lemire.me/blog/2016/06/27/a-fast-alternative-to-the-modulo-operation/) for final index computation instead of bitwise AND. Implementation: Replace lines 204-217 with higher-quality hash (or use a library like xxhash). This reduces collision chain lengths in aggregation hash table, improving find() performance.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~10-20ms reduction (2-5% of 449ms). Reasoning: Hash function quality affects collision rate and thus average probe length in open-addressing table. Poor hash → longer chains → more L1/L2 misses. Better hash → 1-2 fewer probes per lookup on average. Aggregation phase has millions of hash lookups; even 1 fewer probe per lookup = significant savings. Conservative estimate: 2-5% overall improvement.

performance_optimizations.technique: Reorder predicates in lineitem scan to apply most-selective filter first
performance_optimizations.description: Current lineitem scan (lines 438-456) applies two predicates: (1) `l_shipdate_data[i] > cutoff_shipdate` (~33% selectivity), (2) `orders_map.find(orderkey)` (depends on filtered orders cardinality, ~2.2M orders out of 15M = 15% selectivity). Current order: l_shipdate first, then orders_map lookup. Better order: Evaluate orders_map.find() first IF it's cheaper than l_shipdate comparison (no — integer compare is O(1), hash lookup is O(1-3) probes). Current order is already good. However, once zone maps are implemented, can further optimize: skip entire blocks with l_shipdate ≤ cutoff before any per-row processing.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~0-10ms reduction (0-2% of 449ms). Reasoning: Current predicate order is already near-optimal. This optimization is minor and only yields benefit after zone maps are implemented. Included for completeness.

performance_optimizations.technique: Prefetch next group key during aggregation merge to hide memory latency
performance_optimizations.description: Aggregation merge (lines 467-477) iterates over local_aggregates and does hash table lookups/inserts on global_aggregates. This is a cache-friendly pattern but memory latency is not hidden. Optimization: Add `__builtin_prefetch()` to load the next hash table entry before processing current one. Implementation: In the merge loop (line 468-469), prefetch `global_aggregates.table[next_hash_idx]` N instructions ahead of the current lookup.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: ~5-10ms reduction (1-2% of 449ms). Reasoning: Prefetching hides memory latency on modern CPUs. With 300-400 cycle memory latency and ~2GHz CPU, hiding latency on half of lookups = ~0.2-0.3ms per million lookups. Aggregation merge processes ~2M intermediate results; even 0.1-0.2ms saved per ~10K batch adds up. Conservative estimate: 1-2% savings.

summary: Q3 iteration 2 represents a MAJOR IMPROVEMENT over iteration 1: 449ms execution time (down from 1898ms, 4.23x speedup) while maintaining 100% correctness (10 rows match ground truth exactly). The iteration successfully implemented custom open-addressing hash tables (FastHashTable) to replace std::unordered_map, increased thread count to 48, and eliminated nested loop joins (OrderRecord vectors) in favor of direct hash lookups. Results are correct and performance has reached a reasonable baseline. Remaining gap to DuckDB (91ms) is 4.7x, indicating further optimization is warranted. Key bottlenecks: (1) Full-table lineitem scan (60M rows) without block-level zone map filtering (storage design includes zone maps but code doesn't use them), (2) Lack of SIMD vectorization on filtering and arithmetic, (3) Thread count still below maximum hardware capacity (48/64 cores). Recommended optimization sequence: (1) Zone maps to skip ~67% of lineitem blocks (expected 25-40% speedup), (2) SIMD vectorization for filtering/arithmetic (expected 10-15% speedup), (3) Thread tuning to 56-60, (4) Hash function optimization for composite keys. Estimated cumulative impact: 120-180ms from zone maps + 40-70ms from SIMD ≈ 200-250ms total reduction, bringing performance to ~200-250ms range (2.2x closer to DuckDB).
